concepts.name: Three-Part Disruption Test
concepts.definition: A strict diagnostic: (1) the entrant starts in a foothold market such as low-end overserved customers or nonconsumption; (2) the initial product is inferior on mainstream performance attributes; (3) the entrant's performance trajectory improves until it can satisfy the mainstream.

concepts.name: Foothold Market
concepts.definition: The initial customer context where the entrant's different value proposition is good enough or attractive despite mainstream inferiority.

concepts.name: Performance Provided vs. Market Demand
concepts.definition: Technologies often improve faster than customers' ability to use the added performance, allowing simpler and cheaper alternatives to become good enough.

arguments.claim: If one of the three conditions is absent, the case is not disruption in Christensen's strict sense.
arguments.evidence: The theory distinguishes disruption from direct competition, breakthrough invention, or sustaining improvement.

arguments.claim: Oversupply of performance creates the opening for disruption.
arguments.evidence: When mainstream products exceed what customers can use, low-cost entrants with inferior but sufficient performance can compete on convenience, simplicity, price, or access.

cases.name: HDD / excavator / steel synthesis
cases.context: The model generalizes across smaller disk drives, hydraulic excavators, and steel mini-mills.
cases.illustrates: All three start outside mainstream demand, improve along a trajectory, and later invade mainstream markets.
cases.lesson: Use the model as a diagnostic checklist, not a loose label for any fast-growing competitor.

logicChains.description: Find foothold → verify initial mainstream inferiority → compare technology-improvement trajectory to customer-demand trajectory → decide whether the entrant can become good enough before incumbents respond.

terminology.definition: Initial low-end or new-market base where a disruptive entrant can survive.
A market of people who previously lacked access, skill, money, or convenience to consume the product.
A condition where products improve beyond what many customers can use or pay for.

nuance: Disruption is a process, not a moment; early entrants may look irrelevant for years.
The model is intentionally narrow; not every successful entrant is disruptive.
